How To Support Dual Language Learners in the Classroom: 7 Tips
==============================================================

![Image 2: Students in a college course where they can learn how to support dual language learners in the classroom. ](https://www.cityu.edu/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/dual-language-learner-blog-image-1080x617.jpeg)

Dual language learners, or children who are simultaneously developing their native language and English skills, face unique challenges. Navigating new vocabulary in both languages, discovering cultural differences, and struggling with language switching are just a few of the obstacles they face.

With more than [10 million bilingual students](https://www.migrationpolicy.org/programs/data-hub/charts/us-state-profiles-young-dlls) in the United States, knowing how to support dual language learners in the classroom is vital. These tips will enhance your classroom experience and provide a more positive environment for your students.

1. Value the Diverse Linguistic and Cultural Backgrounds
--------------------------------------------------------

When children enter your classroom, they should instantly feel welcome and safe. [International students](https://www.cityu.edu/blog/celebrating-international-students-student-stories/) and dual language learners sometimes feel pressure to fit in and uncomfortable sharing their backgrounds.

To help ease those worries, openly embrace cultural diversity in your classroom. Appreciate and discuss differences in cultural backgrounds, modeling the respect and acceptance that you want students to show one another. Finally, show them that you have a genuine interest in their lives by using teaching examples connected to their cultures.

2. Employ Different Instructional Strategies
--------------------------------------------

Language development looks different for every student, and using multimodal learning activities makes your classroom more inclusive. Plan to use a combination of these activities:

*   Lectures
*   Small group work
*   Whole class discussions
*   Projects
*   Games

In addition to using different types of activities, consider how your instructional strategies incorporate auditory, kinesthetic, and visual elements. Lessons that activate different senses meet the needs of more students, particularly those who struggle with certain learning styles, such as oral lectures.

3. Foster a Collaborative Atmosphere
------------------------------------

In a dual language classroom, the teacher isn’t the only source of support—students should also help one another. Peer and group discussions give students the chance to practice their language skills in a more casual, low-pressure environment than when they speak in front of the class or for assessments. Including flexible seating and collaborative spaces in your dual language classroom setup supports these types of interactions.

Collaboration also helps students feel more connected to their classmates and thus more comfortable making mistakes. When you put students together for group or pair activities, try to use a combination of varied skill levels so they can learn from and help each other.

4. Leverage Visual Aids for Enhanced Understanding
--------------------------------------------------

Research shows that visual aids [help students engage](https://files.eric.ed.gov/fulltext/EJ1143525.pdf) with unfamiliar topics and retain information. They also make it easier to grasp new ideas, access previous knowledge, and gain confidence.

For dual language learners, visual elements cross language barriers and provide them with a recognizable image of an otherwise abstract term. As a result, students can build their vocabulary more quickly and generally feel more interested in learning.

5. Promote Interactive Learning
-------------------------------

Passive, independent learning has its place in a bilingual classroom, but it’s generally not as effective as interactive activities. When students silently read or listen to information, they’re less likely to understand and remember it.

In contrast, interactive learning allows learners to use language in new, creative ways. It also applies vocabulary to realistic contexts they experience in their everyday lives. Some of the activities you can try in your classroom include:

*   Playing games like charades
*   Creating bilingual storybooks or comics
*   Roleplaying scenarios
*   Listening to and discussing music

Breaking up passive instruction with a combination of these strategies makes the learning experience more enjoyable and encourages students to participate.

6. Create Multilingual Resources
--------------------------------

Dual language learners can quickly become discouraged if they can’t understand classroom materials. With multilingual resources, such as bilingual books, they can make connections between their native language and English, improving their literacy skills. At the same time, these resources build their confidence and lessen their anxiety.

Providing students with additional resources in multiple languages also means they can share those materials with family members who don’t speak English. As a result, they can better assist their children and discuss what they’re learning at school.

7. Monitor Progress and Adapt Strategies
----------------------------------------

Think of your classroom as a work in progress, an environment that will change as you learn more about each student’s needs. Administer regular assessments to identify them, but don’t limit yourself to traditional tests and quizzes. Informal assessments are equally—if not more—important and allow for more frequent evaluations of students’ skills.

In addition, provide your students with regular feedback and checklists so they understand their mistakes before they become ingrained. Use assessment methods that incorporate both languages to get the most accurate representation of their abilities.

After assessing students’ progress, make changes based on what you’ve learned. Determine where students need additional support, eliminate unsuccessful methods, and tailor instruction to individual students.

FAQs About Dual Language Learners
---------------------------------

### What are some suggestions to support a dual language learner in your classroom?

You can support dual language learners in your classroom by using both languages in assessments and resources. You can also make learning easier for them by incorporating a variety of teaching strategies and activities into your lessons.

### How do you support bilingual students in the classroom?

Support bilingual students by providing them with materials in both English and their secondary language of instruction. In addition, use visual aids that help with vocabulary development, engagement, and knowledge retention.

### How does a teacher create a positive environment for multi-lingual learners?

To make the classroom environment more welcoming and inclusive, encourage students to talk about their cultures. Show interest in their lives and recognize important events they celebrate, such as holidays.

### What kinds of activities are beneficial for bilingual children?

Interactive and collaborative activities are especially beneficial for bilingual children. In addition to written and oral instruction, have them participate in games, act out scenes, and listen to music to practice their language skills.

Set Up a Dual Language Classroom to Support Your Students
---------------------------------------------------------

Multilingualism becomes more common each year, so learning how to support ESL students in the classroom is critical. Your degree program and professional development experiences can help you better support bilingual children both inside and outside the classroom.

City University of Seattle can get you on a fast-track to a [teacher certification](https://www.cityu.edu/programs-overview/alternative-route-teacher-certification/) with our alternative route program and our dual language program. [Request more information](https://www.cityu.edu/request-information/) to begin your journey toward an education career.

_**Reviewed****by[Melissa Myers](https://www.linkedin.com/in/melissaymyers/?utm\_source=share&utm\_campaign=share\_via&utm\_content=profile&utm\_medium=ios\_app), School of Education and Leadership Enrollment Manager at CityU of Seattle.**_

SUMMARY:
**Learn about and purchase the best books and resources to support young children's learning and development.** **Learn about NAEYC’s informed positions on significant issues affecting young children’s education and development.** **Find a sponsorship opportunity that’s right for you and help support early childhood educators, parents, and other professionals.** **Join NAEYC’s team and help us advance the education of young children across the country.** **Support access to high-quality early childhood education programs and opportunities and resources for educators.** Use these resources to support young children who are learning a second language while continuing to develop their home language. ### Many Languages, One Classroom: Supporting Children in Superdiverse Settings a teacher reading a book to children

FULL CONTENT:
![]()
![]()

# NAEYC

**Find resources that help you in your programs.**

**Find research-based resources, tips and ideas for families—from child development to reading, writing, music, math, and more!**

**Search an ECE degree directory, explore professional standards, and join our community of practice.**

**Engage with our [policy agendas](https://www.naeyc.org/our-work/public-policy-advocacy/federal-and-state-agendas), [advocacy resources](https://www.naeyc.org/get-involved/advocate), and current initiatives.**

**Learn about and purchase the best books and resources to support young children's learning and development.**

**Stay up to date with research-based, teacher-focused articles on birth to age 8 in our award-winning, peer-reviewed journal.**

Read NAEYC's new member-exclusive magazine for early learning community and practice! (Access past issues of [*TYC*](https://www.naeyc.org/publications/156/issues) here.)

**Enhance your career and improve your knowledge, skills, and practices with our [online training](https://www.naeyc.org/resources/pd/online-learning).**

**Explore key early childhood topics such Developmentally Appropriate Practice, play, and math.**

**Stay up-to-date on issues in early childhood education and hear perspectives from a wide range of educators.**

**Learn about NAEYC’s informed positions on significant issues affecting young children’s education and development.**

**[Discover](https://www.naeyc.org/events/annual) the latest research and best practices, and engage with our diverse community of professionals!**

**Ignite and fulfill your professional development goals!**

**Join us at the members-only event and build your advocacy skills, expand your networks, and advance federal and state early childhood policy.**

**Celebrate young children and their families with hands-on activities encouraging movement and healthy lifestyles through music, food, and art.**

**Find a sponsorship opportunity that’s right for you and help support early childhood educators, parents, and other professionals.**

**Deepen your professional knowledge wherever you are with NAEYC’s exciting webinar series and online and face-to-face training opportunities.**

**Discover the [benefits](https://www.naeyc.org/accreditation/early-learning-program-accreditation/whats-new) of early childhood accreditation, learn about the [s](https://www.naeyc.org/accreditation/early-learning/pursuing)[implified process](https://www.naeyc.org/accreditation/early-learning/planned-changes), [find support and resources](https://www.naeyc.org/accreditation/early-learning/support) for your program or [login to the accreditation portal](https://my.naeyc.org).**

**[Login](https://my.naeyc.org) to the Early Learning Accreditation Portal to take charge of your accreditation process.**

**Explore [accreditation](https://www.naeyc.org/accreditation/higher-ed/accreditation) of early childhood higher education programs, discover the accreditation system [standards](https://www.naeyc.org/accreditation/higher-ed/standards), and view a list of [accredited programs](https://www.naeyc.org/accreditation/higher-ed/accredited-programs).**

Access [accreditation data](https://www.naeyc.org/accreditation/accreditation-research-databases) on early learning and higher education program characteristics and quality for research purposes.

**Join your professional membership association, explore [local Affiliates](https://www.naeyc.org/get-involved/membership/affiliates), and access unbeatable member benefits.**

**Explore jobs in the [Career Center](https://www.naeyc.org/get-involved/advance-your-career/career-center) and find [higher education](http://degreefinder.naeyc.org) and [professional development](https://www.naeyc.org/resources/pd) opportunities.**

**Develop grassroots efforts advancing early childhood in your community with these tools and resources.**

**Say [HELLO](http://hello.naeyc.org) and discuss, network, and connect with our interest forums and online communities.**

**Support our mission and reach the NAEYC audience through your advertisement, conference exhibit booth, or sponsorship.**

**Become a leader in your professional association.**

**Read about NAEYC’s [leadership](https://www.naeyc.org/about-us/people/executive-team), [mission, values and beliefs](https://www.naeyc.org/about-us/people/mission-and-strategic-direction), and [strategic governance](https://www.naeyc.org/about-us/people/naeyc-governing-board).**

**Become an organizational or corporate partner to inspire early childhood education at the local and national level.**

**Stay current on all things related to your professional association, from book releases to policy updates.**

**Join NAEYC’s team and help us advance the education of young children across the country.**

**Support access to high-quality early childhood education programs and opportunities and resources for educators.**

**Contact us with your questions-- we're here to help!**

**Popular Searches:**[DAP](https://www.naeyc.org/search/developmentally%20appropriate%20practice); [Coping with COVID-19](https://www.naeyc.org/naeyc.org/conference);[E-books](https://www.naeyc.org/ebooks); [Anti-Bias Education](https://www.naeyc.org/resources/pubs/books/anti-bias-education); [Online Store](https://members.naeyc.org/eweb/DynamicPage.aspx?WebCode=COEPubSearch&pager=12)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Integrating bilingual books and materials enhances understanding for dual language learners, fostering literacy in both languages and providing cultural connections, as highlighted by resources from Language Lizard.

Utilizing translation apps and accessible digital resources enables equitable learning opportunities, allowing diverse students to engage with content in their preferred languages and enhancing communication.
Facilitating discussions that include multicultural perspectives encourages students to share their insights and experiences, promoting empathy and a richer understanding of global literature.
Fostering a safe and welcoming classroom atmosphere encourages students to express their identities and participate actively, vital for their academic and social-emotional growth.
